account.definition:
An Ethereum account is a digital identity on the Ethereum blockchain, allowing users to send, receive Ether, and interact with smart contracts.

Its an objec...
account.translation_note: A digital identity on Ethereum with an address, balance, and optional code.

address.definition:
An Ethereum address is a unique identifier used for receiving tokens, functions similar to a bank account number for cryptocurrencies. Its used to identify your Ethereum account.

It is the ...
address.translation_note: A unique hexadecimal identifier (0x...) for an Ethereum account. NOT a physical location.

cold wallet.translation_note: Frequently translated literally as 'chilled'; loses technical context of offline storage

derivation path.translation_note: Often mistranslated as 'route' or 'way'; critical for wallet recovery guides

elliptic curve digital signature algorit.definition: A cryptographic algorithm used by Ethereum to ensure that funds can only be spent by their owners. It's the preferred method for creating public and private keys. Relevant for account <a href="/glossa...

externally owned account (eoa).definition: Externally Owned Accounts (EOAs) are the most common type of Ethereum account. They are controlled by a person through private keys/recovery phrase. More on Ethereum wallets.

hardware wallet.translation_note: Often mistranslated as 'physical wallet' or 'cold wallet'; needs specific term to distinguish from software-based security

hot wallet.translation_note: Frequently translated literally as 'warm'; loses technical context of internet connectivity

key.definition:
In the context of Ethereum, keys are digital codes: a public key for receiving transactions and a private key for accessing and sending funds.
Public keys: These can be shared openly.
Privat...

keystore.definition: Every account’s private key/address pair exists as a single keyfile in an Ethereum client. These are JSON text files which contains the encrypted private key of the account, which can only be decrypte...

multisig.definition:
Multisig (multi signature) refers to a digital wallet or account that requires multiple signatures or approvals to execute transactions, enhancing security.
This adds extra security compared to t...
multisig.morph_note: Romance languages require gender agreement for signed/signature

passkey.translation_note: Newer WebAuthn standard; needs distinction from passwords and seed phrases

private key.definition: A private key is a secret code that proves you own your digital money and lets you spend it, like a PIN for your account. DO NOT SHARE IT.
private key.translation_note: Secret cryptographic key for signing transactions. NEVER share. Security-critical term.

public key.definition: A public key is a set of characters that lets others send you digital currency securely, like an email address for money.

seed phrase.translation_note: 12 or 24 word sequence to recover a wallet. SECURITY-CRITICAL: must be translated precisely.

signature.morph_note: Romance languages require gender agreement for signed/signature
signature.translation_note: Cryptographic proof that a transaction was authorized by the key holder.

signing.definition: Demonstrating cryptographically that a transaction was approved by the holder of a specific private key.

useroperation.note: `UserOperation` (PascalCase) is a code term for EIP-4337. In prose, use "user operation" (two words, standard casing). Avoid PascalCase outside code context.

wallet.definition: A wallet is a digital tool to store, send, and receive digital currency, like a virtual purse for your online money. More on Ethereum wallets.
wallet.translation_note: Software or hardware for managing crypto keys and assets. NOT a physical wallet.

attestation.morph_note: Ethereum 'attestation' is a specific object; general verb confuses with legal witnessing
attestation.translation_note: A validator's vote on the state of the chain. Specific PoS consensus term.

beacon chain.definition: The Beacon Chain was the blockchain that introduced proof-of-stake and validators to Ethereum. It ran alongside the proof-of-work Ethere...
beacon chain.translation_note: The PoS coordination layer of Ethereum. Named component.

beacon-block.note: A block on the beacon chain containing attestations and the execution payload.

block proposer.definition: The specific validator chosen to create a block in a particular slot.

builder.morph_note: 'Block builder' is specialized MEV role; 'constructing' vs 'software building' differ

casper ffg.definition: Casper-FFG is a proof-of-stake consensus protocol used in conjunction with the LMD-GHOST fork choice algorithm to allow consens...
casper ffg.note: "Casper" is a proper noun. "FFG" = Friendly Finality Gadget. Full: "Casper the Friendly Finality Gadget." Always hyphenated: "Casper-FFG".

churn limit.translation_note: Validator entry/exit queue rate; 'movement limit' or 'rotation limit' too vague

committee.definition: A group of at least 128 validators assigned to validate blocks in each slot. One of the validators in the committee is the aggregator, responsible for aggregating th...

consensus.definition: When more than 2/3 of the computers in a network agree that they have the same set of records, making sure everyone is on the same page. This isn't about the rules they follow, but making sure they al...
consensus.translation_note: Agreement among network nodes on the state of the blockchain.

consensus client.definition: Consensus clients (such as Prysm, Teku, Nimbus, Lighthouse, Lodestar) run Ethereum's proof-of-stake consensus algorithm allowing the network to reach agreement about the h...
consensus client.translation_note: Software implementing Ethereum's proof-of-stake consensus (e.g., Prysm, Lighthouse).

consensus layer.definition: Ethereum's consensus layer is the network of consensus clients.

consensus rules.definition: The block validation rules that full nodes follow to stay in consensus with other nodes. Not to be confused with consensus.

epoch.definition: A period of 32 slots, each slot being 12 seconds, totalling 6.4 minutes. Validator committees are shuffled every epoch for security rea...
epoch.translation_note: A period of 32 slots (~6.4 minutes) in the beacon chain. NOT a geological epoch.

equivocation.definition: A validator sending two messages that contradict each other. One simple example is a transaction sender sending two transactions with the same nonce. Another is a block proposer proposing two blocks a...

execution-payload.note: The transaction-containing portion of a beacon block.

exit.translation_note: A validator leaving the active set. Specific staking term, not a general 'exit'.

finality.definition: Finality is the guarantee that a set of transactions cannot be changed without a huge amount of ETH being lost.
finality.morph_note: 'Finality' is chain property; 'finalized' is block state; must distinguish from 'finishing'

fork choice algorithm.definition: The algorithm used to identify the head of the blockchain. On Ethereum the head of the chain is identified as the fork with the greatest 'weight' of attestations. The weight is the product of the numb...

gasper.note: Combined consensus protocol: Casper FFG + LMD-GHOST. Named algorithm, do not translate.

inactivity leak.translation_note: Gradual loss of stake, not a one-time penalty; must convey progressive nature

issuance.definition: The minting of new ether to reward block proposal, attestation and whistle-blowing.

liquid staking token (lst).definition: A derivative token that represents ownership of the locked cryptocurrency that a user is staking. Upon staking an asset, some platforms allowing minting of liquid staking tokens (LSTs), which represen...

aliases.note: Liquid staking derivative; still widely used

lmd-ghost.definition: The fork-choice algorithm used by Ethereum's consensus clients to identify the head of the chain. LMD-GHOST is an acronym standing for "Latest Message Dr...
lmd-ghost.note: Latest Message Driven Greediest Heaviest Observed SubTree. Does not need spelling out -- "LMD GHOST" is standard. Avoid hyphenation.

aliases.term: Latest Message Driven Greediest Heaviest Observed SubTree

maxeb.note: "EB" uppercase (effective balance). As a whole, standard casing: "maxEB" mid-sentence, "MaxEB" at sentence start. "maximum effective balance" is the spelled-out form.

miner.definition: A network node that finds valid proof-of-work for new blocks, by repeated pass hashing (see Ethash). Miners a...

mining.definition: The process of repeatedly hashing a block header while incrementing a nonce until the result contains an arbitrary number of leading binary zeros. This is the process by...

nothing-at-stake.note: PoS attack vector where validators can vote on multiple chains at no cost.

proof-of-authority.note: Consensus mechanism used in private/test networks. Not used on Ethereum mainnet.

proof-of-stake (pos).definition: A method by which a cryptocurrency blockchain protocol aims to achieve distributed consensus. PoS asks users to prove ownership of a certain amount of cryptocurrency...

aliases.note: May become preferred per content lead -- pending

proposer.morph_note: 'Block proposer' translates differently than general 'to propose an idea'

proposer-boost.note: LMD-GHOST mechanism giving extra weight to timely block proposals.

reorg.note: When the canonical chain changes and previously included blocks are removed.

secret-leader-election.note: Also consider: "secret non-single leader election (SnSLE)" as a related concept.

single-slot-finality.note: Proposed upgrade to finalize blocks in a single slot (~12s) instead of ~15 minutes.

slashing.morph_note: Metaphorical penalty; action vs state require different idioms in TH/HI

slot.definition: A period of time (12 seconds) in which new blocks can be proposed by a validator in the proof-of-stake system. A slot may be empty. 32 s...
slot.translation_note: A 12-second time period in the beacon chain. NOT a physical slot or opening.

social-slashing.note: Community-coordinated penalty for validator misbehavior outside protocol rules.

stake.morph_note: Arabic: verbal noun vs agent noun use distinct patterns. JP/KO: noun is loanword, verb needs native helper
stake.translation_note: Financial deposit (noun) or the act of depositing ETH (verb) to participate in consensus. NOT a wooden stake.
stake.note: Forms: "stake" (n/v), "staked" (adj/past), "staking" (gerund), "staker" (agent). "The staker used ETH as stake and staked it in the network."

stake-grinding.note: Attack where validators manipulate randomness to gain advantage in block proposal.

staking.definition: Depositing a quantity of ether (your stake) to become a validator and secure the network. A validator checks
staking.morph_note: Arabic: verbal noun vs agent noun use distinct patterns. JP/KO: noun is loanword, verb needs native helper
staking.translation_note: The act of depositing ETH to participate in consensus. Gerund of 'stake'.

supermajority.definition: Supermajority is the term given for an amount exceeding 2/3 (66%) of the total staked ether securing Ethereum. A supermajority vote is required for blocks to be <a href="/glossary/#finality">finalized...

sync committee.definition: A sync committee is a randomly selected group of validators that refresh every ~27 hours. Their purpose is to add their signatures to valid block headers. Sync commi...

validator.definition: A node in a proof-of-stake system responsible for storing data, processing transactions, and adding new blocks to the blockchain. To activate...
validator.morph_note: CN/VI: technical 'validation' vs 'validator' role uses different semantic registers
validator.translation_note: Software entity (node) that validates blocks in proof-of-stake. Sometimes refers to the human operator. NOT the same as 'miner' (PoW).

validator lifecycle.definition:
The sequence of states that a validator can exist in. These include:

  • deposited: At least 32 ETH has been deposited to the deposit contract b...

weak-subjectivity.note: PoS concept: new nodes need a recent trusted checkpoint to sync safely. Abstract concept, hard to translate.

withdrawal.morph_note: Action vs resulting transaction are grammatically distinct in Arabic
withdrawal.translation_note: Removing staked ETH from a validator. Specific to PoS staking context.

withdrawal-credentials.note: Staking configuration specifying where withdrawn ETH goes.

bls.note: Boneh-Lynn-Shacham digital signature scheme. Generally not spelled out in use -- "BLS" alone is fine and widely recognized.

bls-signature.note: Boneh-Lynn-Shacham signature scheme. 'BLS' stays Latin, 'signature' translates.

commitment.translation_note: Mathematical binding (e.g., KZG commitment); 'promise' or 'obligation' misses the cryptographic meaning

cryptography.definition:
It is the practice of securing communication and data through the use of codes, so that only those for whom the information is intended can read and process it.
It involves techniques for encrypt...

dagger-hashimoto.note: Predecessor to Ethash mining algorithm. Historical, named algorithm.

distributed hash table (dht).definition: A data structure containing `(key, value)` pairs used by Ethereum nodes to identify peers to connect to and determine which protocols to use to communicate.

encryption.definition: Encryption is the conversion of electronic data into a form unreadable by anyone except the owner of the correct decryption key.

entropy.definition: In the context of cryptography, lack of predictability or level of randomness. When generating secret information, such as private keys, algorithms usually rely on...

ethash.definition: A proof-of-work algorithm that was used on Ethereum before it transitioned to proof-of-stake.

hash.definition: A fixed-length fingerprint of variable-size input, produced by a hash function. (See keccak-256).
hash.morph_note: Usually loanword noun; verb often 'calculate the checksum' in formal docs
hash.translation_note: A fixed-length output from a cryptographic function. Also used as verb ('to hash data').

hash rate.definition: The number of hash calculations made per second by computers running mining software.
hash rate.morph_note: Usually loanword noun; verb often 'calculate the checksum' in formal docs

hashing.morph_note: Usually loanword noun; verb often 'calculate the checksum' in formal docs

keccak-256.definition: Cryptographic hash function used in Ethereum. Keccak-256 was standardized as SHA-3.
keccak-256.note: "Keccak" capitalized in copy (proper noun). In code: `keccak256` (lowercase, no hyphen).

kzg.note: Kate-Zaverucha-Goldberg commitment scheme. Also referred to as "Kate Commitment Scheme." Generally not spelled out -- "KZG" alone is recognized.

merkle proof.note: "Merkle" always capitalized (named after Ralph Merkle). "proof" standard casing.

merkle root.definition: A Merkle root is the single top hash of a Merkle tree. It verifies all transactions within a block.

network hashrate.definition: The collective hashrate produced by an entire mining network. Mining on Ethereum was switched off when Ethereum moved to proof-of-stake.

secure hash algorithm (sha).definition: A family of cryptographic hash functions published by the National Institute of Standards and Technology (NIST).

trusted setup.translation_note: One-time initialization phase (e.g., KZG ceremony); needs consistent term

apr.definition: APR, or Annual Percentage Rate, reflects the yearly cost of borrowing money, including interest and fees, as a percentage.

chainlink.note: Oracle network. Product name -- do NOT translate as 'chain link'.

de-peg.definition: When a stablecoin loses its fixed exchange rate to its reference asset

decentralized exchange (dex).definition: A type of Ethereum app that lets you swap tokens with peers on the network. DEXes are not subject to geographical restrictions like centralized exchanges – anyone can participate.

decentralized finance (defi).definition: A broad category of Ethereum apps aiming to provide financial services backed by the blockchain, without any intermediaries. More on decentralized finance (DeFi)
decentralized finance (defi).translation_note: Financial services on blockchain without intermediaries. 'DeFi' stays Latin.

index.definition: A network structure meant to optimize the querying of information from across the blockchain by providing an efficient path to its storage source.

liquidity.definition: Liquidity is how quickly and easily an asset can be converted into cash or another asset. Decentralized exchanges like Uniswap have multiple liquidity pools where asset holders can deposit their asset...
liquidity.translation_note: How easily an asset can be traded. DeFi-specific meaning: assets in a pool.

liquidity tokens.definition: Liquidity tokens (LST) are digital tokens issued to participants who deposit assets into a liquidity pool, which is a collection of funds locked in a smart contract and used to facilitate trading on a...

oracle.definition: An oracle is a bridge between the blockchain and the real world. They act as onchain APIs that can be queried for information and used ...
oracle.translation_note: A service providing external data to smart contracts. NOT a mythological oracle.

oracle-problem.note: The challenge of getting reliable external data onto a blockchain.

overcollateralization.translation_note: A mouthful in most languages; 'too much security' is common but wrong

peg.definition: A fixed exchange rate between a token and a reference asset (e.g., 1 USDC = 1 USD)
peg.translation_note: Highly idiomatic; translating as physical 'hook' or 'pin' is confusing in financial context

price impact.translation_note: Distinct from slippage but often conflated in translations

slippage.translation_note: One of the most inconsistently translated terms; 'sliding' or 'deviation' common but wrong

stablecoin.definition: A stablecoin is a type of cryptocurrency designed to have a stable value, often pegged to a currency or commodity (like US dollar), to minimize price volatility. <a href="/stablecoins/">More on stable...

swap.morph_note: DE/NL: noun (Tausch) and verb (tauschen) differ; DeFi context needs technical synonyms

api.translation_note: Application Programming Interface. Technical acronym, keep Latin.

decentralized application (dapp).definition:
A dApp is a decentralized application that runs on a blockchain network, offering services without a central controlling authority. More on decentralized applications.

aliases.note: Lowercase as regular noun. Prefer 'app' or 'onchain app' for new content.

decentralized application (dapp).note: Prefer 'app', 'application', or qualify with 'onchain'/'on Ethereum' for new content. E.g., 'Build an onchain app' over 'Build a dapp'.
decentralized application (dapp).translation_note: Application running on a blockchain. Prefer 'app' or 'onchain app' for new content.

devp2p.note: Ethereum networking protocol stack. Technical identifier.

discv5.note: Node discovery protocol v5. Technical identifier.

echidna.note: Fuzzing tool. Product name -- do NOT translate as the animal.

faucet.definition: A service carried out via smart contract that dispenses funds in the form of free test ether that can be used on a testnet.

kurtosis.note: Development and testing platform. Product name.

libp2p.note: Modular networking library used by consensus clients. Technical identifier.

manticore.note: Symbolic execution tool. Product name -- do NOT translate as the mythical creature.

openzeppelin.note: Smart contract library and security framework. Product name.

portal-network.note: Lightweight protocol for serving Ethereum data to resource-constrained devices.

slither.note: Static analysis tool. Product name -- do NOT translate as the verb 'to slither'.

web3.definition: Web3 is the new internet with blockchain, where users control their data and transactions, not companies. No need to share any personal information. More on web3.
web3.translation_note: The vision of a decentralized internet built on blockchain. Proper noun-like, often kept as 'Web3'.

eth1.definition: 'Eth1' is a term that referred to Mainnet Ethereum, the existing proof-of-work blockchain. This term has since been deprecated in favor of the 'execution layer'. <a href="https://blog.ethereum.org/202...
eth1.note: Legacy term. Preferred: 'execution layer'. Deprecated by ecosystem consensus; only used in historical context.

eth2.definition: 'Eth2' is a term that referred to a set of Ethereum protocol upgrades, including Ethereum's transition to proof-of-stake. This term has since been deprecated in favor of the 'consensus layer'. <a href...
eth2.note: Legacy term. Preferred: 'consensus layer'. Deprecated by ecosystem consensus; only used in historical context.

ether.definition: The native cryptocurrency of Ethereum, commonly referred to as “ETH”. It is used to cover transaction fees when using Ethereum ecosystem and applications. More on ether.
ether.note: Denomination of ether (lowercase). Named after the concept of luminiferous aether. ETH is the ticker symbol (always Latin, uppercase). "ether" is the currency name (lowercase).
ether.translation_note: The native cryptocurrency of Ethereum. Lowercase when referring to the currency. NOT the chemical ether.

go ethereum (geth).definition: Go Ethereum. One of the most prominent implementations of the Ethereum protocol, written in Go. Read more at geth.ethereum.org

anti-sybil.definition: Are ways to stop people from pretending to be many users at once on the internet, ensuring each user is a real, separate person. This helps keep online interactions fair and honest.
anti-sybil.note: "anti" lowercase, "Sybil" capitalized (proper noun from the book "Sybil").

asic.definition: Application-specific integrated circuit. This usually refers to an integrated circuit, custom-built for cryptocurrency mining.

besu.note: Java-based execution client (Hyperledger). Product name, do not translate meaning.

big-endian.definition: A positional number representation where the most significant digit is first in memory. The opposite of little-endian, where the least significant digit is first.

bitcoin.note: Context-dependent: "Bitcoin" (capital B) for the network/protocol/system. "bitcoin" (lowercase b) for the currency unit. Default to capital B.

block.definition: A block is where transactions or digital actions are stored. Once a block is full, it gets linked to the previous one, creating a chain of blocks or a "blockchain". <a href="/developers/docs/blocks/">...
block.translation_note: A unit of bundled transactions added to the blockchain. NOT a physical block or obstruction.

block explorer.definition: An interface that allows a user to search for information from, and about, a blockchain. This includes retrieving individual transactions, activity associated with specific addresses and information a...

block header.definition: The block header is a collection of metadata about a block and a summary of the transactions included in the execution payload.

block propagation.definition: The process of transmitting a confirmed block to all other nodes in the network.

block reward.definition: The amount of ether rewarded to the proposer of a new valid block.

block status.definition:
The states that a block can exist in. The possible states include:

  • proposed: the block was proposed by a validator
  • scheduled: validators are currently submitting data
  • ...

block time.definition: The time interval between blocks being added to the blockchain.

block validation.definition: The process of checking that a new block contains valid transactions and signatures, builds on the heaviest historical chain (meaning the one that has accumulated the most attestations in its history)...

blockchain.definition:
A blockchain is a database of transactions, duplicated and shared on all computers in the network, ensuring data cannot be altered retroactively.

blockchain.translation_note: A distributed ledger of linked blocks. The entire database of transactions.

chain.morph_note: Prefixes 'on-'/'off-' become complex prepositional phrases in TR/HU

client-diversity.note: Principle of running multiple client implementations to prevent single points of failure.

compiling.definition: Converting code written in a high-level programming language (e.g., Solidity) into a lower-level language (e.g., EVM bytecode).

computational infeasibility.definition: A process is computationally infeasible if it would take an impracticably long time (e.g., billions of years) to do it for anyone who might conceivably have an interest in carrying it out.

considered for inclusion (cfi).definition: A Core EIP which is not yet active on Mainnet, and client developers are generally positive towards the idea. Assuming it meets all the requirements for mainnet inclusion,...

crypto.translation_note: Short for cryptocurrency or cryptography depending on context.

decentralization.definition: The concept of moving the control and execution of processes away from a central entity.

decentralized.translation_note: Distributed across many participants with no central authority.

decentralized science (desci).definition: DeSci, or Decentralized Science, is a movement applying blockchain technology to scientific research. It leverages DAOs,

discovery.definition: The process by which an Ethereum node finds other nodes to connect to.

erigon.note: Go-based execution client. Product name, do not translate meaning.

ethereum.translation_note: The blockchain network itself. Proper noun, always transliterate in non-Latin scripts.

execution client.definition: Execution clients (formerly known as "Eth1 clients"), such as Besu, Erigon, Go-Ethereum (Geth), Nethermind, are tasked with processing and broadcasting transactions and managing Ethereum's state. They...

execution layer.definition: Ethereum's execution layer is the network of execution clients.

finalized.morph_note: 'Finality' is chain property; 'finalized' is block state; must distinguish from 'finishing'

finney.definition: A denomination of ether. 1 finney = 1015 wei. 103 finney = 1 ether.
finney.note: Denomination of ether (lowercase). Named after Hal Finney (proper noun when referring to the person).

fork.definition: A change in protocol causing the creation of an alternative chain.
fork.morph_note: Often loanword as noun but translated as 'divergence' as verb in RU/PL
fork.translation_note: A change in protocol creating an alternative chain. NOT an eating utensil.

fraud proof.definition: A security model for certain layer 2 solutions where, to increase speed, transactions are rolled up into batches and submitted to Ethe...

frontier.definition: The initial test development stage of Ethereum, which lasted from July 2015 to March 2016.

genesis block.definition: The first block in a blockchain, used to initialize a particular network and its cryptocurrency.

hard fork.morph_note: Often loanword as noun but translated as 'divergence' as verb in RU/PL

history-expiry.note: Mechanism to remove old block history from nodes. Distinct from state expiry.

integrated development environment (ide).definition: A user interface that typically combines a code editor, compiler, runtime, and debugger. More on integrated development environments.

layer 1 (l1).definition: Layer 1 refers to the main blockchain in a multi-level blockchain network. For example, Ethereum and Bitcoin are layer one blockchains. Many layer two blockchain offload resource-intense transactions ...
layer 1 (l1).translation_note: The base blockchain (e.g., Ethereum mainnet). Lowercase, not title-cased.

lighthouse.note: Rust-based consensus client. Product name -- do NOT translate as 'lighthouse/beacon tower'.

lodestar.note: TypeScript-based consensus client. Product name -- do NOT translate as 'guiding star'.

mainnet.definition: Short for "main network," this is the main public Ethereum blockchain.
mainnet.translation_note: The primary Ethereum network (as opposed to testnets). Proper noun, capitalize.
mainnet.note: When referring to Ethereum Mainnet specifically, treat as proper noun (capitalize). When referring to other mainnets generically, standard casing.

message.definition: An internal transaction that is never serialized and only sent within the EVM.

metamask.note: Wallet software. Product name -- do NOT translate as 'Meta Mask' or equivalent.

nethermind.note: .NET-based execution client. Product name, do not translate meaning.

network.definition: Referring to the Ethereum network, a peer-to-peer network that propagates transactions and blocks to every Ethereum node (network participant). <a href="/developers/docs/networks/">More on networks</a...

nimbus.note: Nim-based consensus client. Product name -- do NOT translate as 'cloud/halo'.

node.definition: A software client that participates in the network. More on nodes and clients.
node.translation_note: A computer running Ethereum client software. NOT a mathematical vertex or physical junction.

off-ramp.definition: A service that converts cryptocurrency to fiat currency

offchain.definition: Offchain means any transaction or data that exists outside the blockchain. Because committing every transaction onchain can be expensive and inefficient, third-party tools like oracles that handle pri...
offchain.translation_note: Not recorded on the blockchain. Written without hyphen (like 'offline'). Adjective.

ommer (uncle) block.note: Gender-neutral term replacing Bitcoin-originated "uncle block." Prefer "ommer."

on-ramp.definition: A service that converts fiat currency to cryptocurrency
on-ramp.translation_note: One of the hardest metaphors to translate; literal 'ramp' translations are confusing

onboarding.translation_note: Often translated as 'training' or 'hiring'; in crypto means user's first wallet/app interaction

onchain.definition:
Refers to actions or transactions that happen on the blockchain and are publicly available.

Think of it as writing something in a big, shared notebook that everyone can see and check, makin...
onchain.translation_note: Recorded on the blockchain. Written without hyphen (like 'online'). Adjective.

peer.definition: Connected computers running Ethereum client software that have identical copies of the blockchain.
peer.translation_note: A connected computer in the network. Technical networking term, not a social equal.

permissioned.translation_note: Must contrast clearly with 'permissionless'; pair should be standardized together

permissionless.definition: Permissionless means anyone can join and use a system like Ethereum. It's open for everyone to participate and doesn't require any approval.

privacy.translation_note: In Ethereum context: protecting transaction and identity data on a public blockchain.

private chain.definition: A fully private blockchain is one with permissioned access, not publicly available for use.

proof-of-work (pow).definition: A security mechanism for blockchains that requires nodes to expend energy in the form of computation to find a certain value.

protocol.translation_note: The set of rules governing the Ethereum network. NOT a medical or diplomatic protocol.

prysm.note: Go-based consensus client. Product name, do not translate meaning.

public goods.definition: Public goods are things everyone can use for free, like parks or clean air, and using them doesn’t stop others from using them too. Governments often provide these because businesses usually won’t, si...

re-entrancy attack.definition: An attack that consists of an attacker contract calling a victim contract function in such a way that during execution the victim calls the attacker contract again, recursively. This can result, for e...

recursive length prefix (rlp).definition: An encoding standard designed by the Ethereum developers to encode and serialize objects (data structures) of arbitrary complexity and lengt...

reth.note: Rust-based execution client. Product name, do not translate meaning.

reward.definition: An amount of ether awarded to validators that perform certain functions, including proposing a block or participating in a...

sepolia.note: Ethereum testnet. Network name, keep Latin.

serialization.definition: The process of converting a data structure into a sequence of bytes.

snark.definition: Short for "succinct non-interactive argument of knowledge", a SNARK is a type of zero-knowledge proof. More on zero-kno...

soft fork.definition: A divergence in a blockchain that occurs when the consensus rules change. Contrary to a hard...
soft fork.morph_note: Often loanword as noun but translated as 'divergence' as verb in RU/PL

stark.definition: Short for "scalable transparent argument of knowledge", a STARK is a type of zero-knowledge proof. More on zero-knowled...

state bloat.definition: Growth of the Ethereum state database making it harder for nodes to store and process
state bloat.translation_note: 'Bloat' translated as 'swelling' or 'illness' in many languages

state-expiry.note: Mechanism to remove old state data from nodes to reduce storage requirements.

statelessness.definition: A design goal where nodes don't need to store the full state to validate blocks
statelessness.translation_note: Must not imply 'no status' or 'no country'

sybil attack.definition: Sybil attacks refer to individual humans tricking a system into thinking they are multiple people to increase their influence.

syncing.definition: The process of downloading the entire latest version of a blockchain to a node.
syncing.morph_note: Long formal word for noun vs short English-derived slang for verb/action

szabo.definition: A denomination of ether. 1 szabo = 1012 wei. 106 szabo = 1 ether.
szabo.note: Denomination of ether (lowercase). Named after Nick Szabo (proper noun when referring to the person).

teku.note: Java-based consensus client. Product name, do not translate meaning.

terminal total difficulty (ttd).definition: The total difficulty is the sum of the Ethash mining difficulty for all blocks up to some specific point in the blockchain. The terminal total difficulty is a specific value for the total difficulty t...

testnet.definition: Short for "test network," a network used to simulate the behavior of the main Ethereum network.
testnet.translation_note: A test network for development. Opposite of mainnet.

trust assumptions.definition: Trust assumptions are basic beliefs about a system's safety and dependability, guiding what we trust for the system to function.

trust-minimized.translation_note: More precise than 'trustless'; 'low trust' sounds like a negative attribute

turing complete.definition: A concept named after English mathematician and computer scientist Alan Turing - a system of data-manipulation rules (such as a computer's instruction set, a programming language, or a cellular automa...
turing complete.note: Turing is always capitalized (proper noun from Alan Turing).

web 3.0.note: Alias for Web3. Prefer "web3" (standard casing) for new content. "Web 3.0" is acceptable but not preferred.

web2.definition: Is the current internet, focused on user-generated content and social media that are controlled by few companies. Web3 is a crypto belief that users should control their data and transactions instead.

whitepaper.note: "Ethereum Whitepaper" is a proper noun (published work). A general "whitepaper" is standard casing.

witness.note: In Verkle tree context: proof data needed to verify a state access without full state.

yellow paper.note: "Ethereum Yellow Paper" is a proper noun (published work). A general "yellow paper" is standard casing.

decentralized autonomous organization (d.definition: A DAO is a digital organization run by rules coded on a blockchain, where decisions are made by member votes, not a central authority. <a href="/dao/">More on decentralized autonomous organizations (D...
decentralized autonomous organization (d.translation_note: Organization governed by smart contract rules and member votes. 'DAO' stays Latin.

ethereum improvement proposal (eip).definition: A design document providing information to the Ethereum community, describing a proposed new feature or its processes or environment (see ERC). Introducti...
ethereum improvement proposal (eip).note: All EIP-n identifiers (EIP-1559, EIP-4844, EIP-7702, etc.) ALWAYS stay in Latin script. Never translate or transliterate the numbered identifier. The expanded form (Ethereum Improvement Proposal) can be translated for explanation.
ethereum improvement proposal (eip).translation_note: EIP-n identifiers always Latin. Expanded form translatable. Pattern applies to ALL numbered EIPs.

proposal.morph_note: 'Block proposer' translates differently than general 'to propose an idea'

quadratic voting.definition: Is a voting method where voters express how strongly they feel about issues. It allows voters to show not just preference, but also the intensity of their preference.

vote.morph_note: Governance: 'ballot'(n) vs 'act of voting'(v) use different stems in Finno-Ugric

ethereum name service (ens).definition: Ethereum Name Service is like an internet phonebook for Ethereum addresses. Instead of using long  wallet addresses, ENS lets you use simple names like "john.eth" to send and receive digital money and...

stealth-address.note: Privacy technique generating one-time addresses for each transaction.

maximal extractable value (mev).definition: The maximum value that can be extracted from block production in excess of the standard block reward and gas fees by including, excluding, and changing the order of transactions in a block. <a href="/...
maximal extractable value (mev).translation_note: Value extracted by reordering/inserting transactions in a block. 'MEV' stays Latin.

mev-boost.note: Software for proposer-builder separation. Product name, do not translate.

byzantium fork.definition: The first of two hard forks for the Metropolis development stage. It included EIP-649 Metropolis

constantinople fork.definition: The second part of the Metropolis stage, originally planned for mid-2018. Expected to include a switch to a hybrid proof-of-work/

ethereum request for comments (erc).definition:
ERC (Ethereum Request for Comments) is a type of technical documentation used in the Ethereum community to propose new standards of usage for the Ethereum network.

These pr...
ethereum request for comments (erc).note: All ERC-n identifiers (ERC-20, ERC-721, ERC-1155, etc.) ALWAYS stay in Latin script. Never translate or transliterate the numbered identifier. The expanded form (Ethereum Request for Comments) can be translated for explanation.
ethereum request for comments (erc).translation_note: ERC-n identifiers always Latin. Expanded form translatable. Pattern applies to ALL numbered ERCs.

fusaka.note: Planned Ethereum upgrade combining Fulu and Osaka. Portmanteau name.

homestead.definition: The second development stage of Ethereum, launched in March 2016 at block 1,150,000.

pectra.note: Ethereum upgrade combining Prague (execution) and Electra (consensus). Portmanteau name.

shapella.note: Ethereum upgrade combining Shanghai (execution) and Capella (consensus). Enabled staking withdrawals.

the merge.translation_note: The 2022 upgrade transitioning Ethereum from proof-of-work to proof-of-stake. Named event.

batching.definition: Grouping multiple L2 transactions together for submission to L1
batching.translation_note: 'Stacking' or 'grouping' miss the technical efficiency aspect

blob.translation_note: Binary large object. In Ethereum: data attached to transactions for L2 scaling (EIP-4844).

bridge.definition: A blockchain bridge is used to transfer assets from one blockchain network to another. For example you can use bridge to transfer ETH from the main Ethereum network to cheaper Layer 2 scaling solution...
bridge.morph_note: Physical bridge noun vs functional/computing verb; HU/FI agglutinative suffixes change
bridge.translation_note: Infrastructure for transferring assets between blockchains (noun). Also used as verb ('to bridge tokens'). NOT a physical bridge.

dac.note: Data availability committee. Not to be confused with "decentralized autonomous corporation" (rare usage). DAO is the more common term for that concept.

data availability.definition: Any node can independently verify transactions on a blockchain in order to maintain transparency and trust in the system.
data availability.translation_note: The guarantee that block data is accessible for verification.

data-availability-sampling.note: Technique allowing nodes to verify data availability without downloading entire blobs.

erasure-coding.note: Data redundancy technique used in data availability sampling.

escape hatch.definition: Mechanism allowing users to withdraw from an L2 if the sequencer fails
escape hatch.translation_note: Literal translations sound like a physical emergency exit

fault proof.definition: Modern term for fraud proof used by optimistic rollups
fault proof.translation_note: Needs standardization alongside existing 'fraud proof' entry

forced withdrawal.definition: An L2-to-L1 withdrawal that bypasses the sequencer
forced withdrawal.translation_note: Must sound like a 'protocol right' not an 'aggressive act'

layer 2 (l2).definition: Layer 2s are another networks built on top of Ethereum main network to make transactions faster and cheaper. More on layer 2.
layer 2 (l2).translation_note: Networks built on top of Ethereum for scaling. Lowercase, not title-cased.

optimistic rollup.definition: Optimistic Rollup is a Layer 2 solution that speeds up transactions on Ethereum, assuming they're valid by default unless challenged. <a href="/developers/docs/scaling/optimistic-rollups/">More on Opt...
optimistic rollup.translation_note: L2 scaling solution assuming transactions are valid unless challenged.

peerdas.note: Peer Data Availability Sampling. Protocol name, do not translate.

plasma.definition: An offchain scaling solution that uses fraud proofs, like optimistic rollups. Plasma is limited to simple transactions l...

proto-danksharding.definition: A new transaction type which accepts "blobs" of data for Ethereum. This "blob" data is temporarily stored on the beacon chain for 4096 epochs (~18.2 days), and can optionally be pruned after to help r...

rollup.translation_note: Layer 2 scaling technology that bundles transactions. Always a noun, never used as a verb in Ethereum.

rollups.definition: A type of layer 2 scaling solution that batches multiple transactions and submits them to the Ethereum main chain in a single transact...

sequencer.definition: A sequencer is a program responsible for ordering transactions in a blockchain network, particularly within Layer 2 scaling solutions.

settlement.definition: The process by which L2 transactions achieve finality on L1
settlement.translation_note: 'Payment' or 'agreement' miss the finality meaning; L2-specific context

sidechain.definition: A scaling solution that uses a separate chain with different, often faster, consensus rules. A bridge is needed to connect these sidechains to
sidechain.morph_note: Prefixes 'on-'/'off-' become complex prepositional phrases in TR/HU

state channels.definition: A layer 2 solution where a channel is set up between participants, where they can transact freely and cheaply. Only a transaction ...

throughput.definition: The number of transactions a network can process per unit of time
throughput.translation_note: 'Performance' or 'yield' too broad; must convey transactions-per-second specifically

validity proof.definition: A security model for certain layer 2 solutions where, to increase speed, transactions are rolled up into batches and submitted to Ethereum in a single transaction. The...

validium.definition: An offchain solution that uses validity proofs to improve transaction throughput. Unlike Zero-knowledge rollups, validium dat...

verifier.morph_note: ZK contexts distinguish heavily between prover and verifier; must be non-interchangeable

zero-knowledge proof.definition: A zero-knowledge proof is a cryptographic method that allows an individual to prove that a statement is true without conveying any additional information. <a href="/developers/docs/scaling/zk-rollups/...
zero-knowledge proof.translation_note: Cryptographic proof that something is true without revealing the underlying data.
zero-knowledge proof.note: ZKP is the common acronym. ZK-proof (hyphenated, lowercase) is the accepted short form. Follow first-mention rule: spell out then use acronym.

zero-knowledge rollup.definition: A rollup of transactions that use validity proofs to offer increased layer 2 transaction throu...

zkevm.note: Zero-Knowledge Ethereum Virtual Machine. Technical identifier, keep Latin.

application binary interface (abi).definition: A JSON file that defines the functions and variables included in a smart contract. The ABI allows bytecode to be mapped into human-readable formats.

bytecode.definition: Code expressed in a compact, numeric form so that it can be executed efficiently by the EVM.

call data.definition: Data sent along with a transaction to a smart contract
call data.translation_note: 'Phone data' or 'shouting data' mistranslations in some languages
call data.note: "call data" in prose (two words). In code: `calldata` (lowercase) or `CALLDATA` (uppercase). These are distinct representations of the same concept.

contract.translation_note: Smart contract -- code deployed on the blockchain. NOT a legal agreement.

contract account.definition: An account containing code that executes whenever it receives a transaction from another account (EOA]

contract creation transaction.definition: A special transaction that includes a contract's initiation code. The recipient is set to `null` and the contract is deployed to an address generated from the user...

deploy.translation_note: To publish a smart contract to the blockchain. Similar to software deployment.

deposit contract.definition: The gateway to staking on Ethereum. The deposit contract is a smart contract on Ethereum that accepts deposits of ETH and manages validator balances. A validator cannot be activated without depositing...

ethereum virtual machine (evm).definition: A stack-based virtual machine that executes bytecode. In Ethereum, the execution model specifies how the system state is altered given a series of bytecode instructio...
ethereum virtual machine (evm).translation_note: Stack-based runtime that executes smart contract bytecode. 'EVM' stays Latin, full name translates.

event.translation_note: In Solidity: a logging mechanism in smart contracts. NOT a calendar event.

evm assembly language.definition: A human-readable form of EVM <a href="/glossary/#bytecode">bytecode.
evm assembly language.note: EVM follows acronym casing within this term.

fallback function.definition: A default function called in the absence of data or a declared function name.

formal-verification.note: Mathematical proof that smart contract code meets its specification. Academic term.

gas optimization.definition: The practice of writing efficient smart contract code to minimize gas costs
gas optimization.translation_note: 'Making gas better' is a common mistranslation; means reducing costs

immutable deployed code problem.definition: Once a contract's (or library's) code is deployed, it becomes immutable. Standard software development practices rely on being able to fix possible bugs and add...

library.definition: A special type of contract that has no payable functions, no fallback function, and no data storage. Therefore, it cannot receive or hold ether, or store data. ...
library.translation_note: In Solidity: reusable deployed code that other contracts can call. NOT a book library.

log.translation_note: In Solidity: data emitted by events. NOT a physical log or diary.

opcode.translation_note: Operation code -- a single instruction in the EVM. Keep Latin in code contexts.

precompile.definition: Specialized contract built into the EVM at a fixed address
precompile.translation_note: Must avoid confusion with 'pre-compiled code' in general software development

reentrancy guard.definition: A mechanism to prevent reentrancy attacks in smart contracts
reentrancy guard.translation_note: Often translated as 're-entry shield'; needs precise security term

require.translation_note: Solidity keyword that checks conditions in smart contracts. Keep as-is in code contexts.

singleton.definition: A computer programming term that describes an object of which only a single instance can exist.

smart contract.definition: A smart contract is a program that automatically executes agreements on a blockchain, like a self-enforcing digital contract. Introduction to smart contracts.
smart contract.translation_note: Self-executing code deployed on Ethereum. Combines 'smart' (automated) + 'contract' (agreement encoded as code).

solidity.definition: A procedural (imperative) programming language with syntax that is similar to JavaScript, C++, or Java. The most popular and most frequently used language for Ethereum <a href="/glossary/#smart-contra...
solidity.translation_note: Programming language for Ethereum smart contracts. Proper noun, keep Latin.

solidity inline assembly.definition: EVM assembly language in a Solidity program. Solidity's support for inline assembly makes it easier to write certain operations.

source-code-verification.note: Process of proving deployed bytecode matches published source code.

state.definition: A snapshot of all balances and data at a particular point in time on the blockchain, normally referring to the condition at a particular block.

state-trie.note: Merkle Patricia trie storing all Ethereum account state.

storage-trie.note: Per-account trie storing contract storage data.

vyper.definition: A high-level programming language with Python-like syntax. Intended to get closer to a pure functional language. Created by Vitalik Buterin. <a href="/developers/docs/smart-contracts/languages/#vyper"...

claim.definition: The act of collecting tokens from an airdrop, reward, or vesting contract
claim.translation_note: Transitions from action (verb) to right (noun) to status (adjective); requires distinct forms
claim.morph_note: Action(v) to right(n) to status(adj); each needs specific phrasing in Asian languages

fractionalization.definition: Dividing a single NFT or asset into multiple smaller, tradeable units

lp token.note: LP = liquidity provider (acronym, always uppercase). Token is standard.

mint.definition: Minting is the process of creating new tokens and bringing them into circulation so that they can be used. It's a decentralized mechanism to create a new token without the involvement of the central a...
mint.morph_note: Lacks direct financial equivalent in non-Western languages

minting.morph_note: Lacks direct financial equivalent in non-Western languages

non-fungible token (nft).definition: A unique digital item you can own, like art or collectibles, verified by blockchain technology. More on non-fungible tokens (NFTs).
non-fungible token (nft).translation_note: Unique digital token proving ownership. 'NFT' stays Latin in most contexts.

poap.definition: Proof of Attendance Protocol is used to create a digital collectible (NFT) that proves you attended a specific event or activity.
poap.note: Proof of Attendance Protocol. Product/project name. Does not need to be spelled out -- "POAP" is widely recognized.

token.definition: A tradable virtual good defined in smart contracts on the Ethereum blockchain.
token.translation_note: A digital asset on Ethereum following a standard (ERC-20, ERC-721). NOT a physical coin or voucher.

token factory.definition: A token factory is a smart contract that facilitates the creation of tokens within a specific standard, such as ERC-20, ERC-721, or ERC...

token uri.note: URI = universal resource identifier (acronym, always uppercase in copy). Code usage may differ (tokenURI, token_uri).

transfer.translation_note: Moving tokens between addresses. In ERC-20 context, a specific function name.

vesting.definition: A schedule by which tokens are gradually released to their owners over time
vesting.translation_note: Often translated as 'clothing' (from Latin root) or 'investment'

wrapped token.definition: A blockchain-based token that represents another cryptocurrency or asset on a different network. For example, Wrapped Ether (WETH) represents Ether (ETH) in a...

base fee.definition: Every block has a reserve price known as the 'base fee'. It is the minimum gas fee a user must pay to include a transaction in the next bloc...

blob-fee.note: Fee for blob data in EIP-4844 transactions. Separate from regular gas fees.

gas.definition: Gas is the fee paid for transactions and smart contracts on a blockchain, like Ethereum. More on gas and fees.
gas.translation_note: Unit measuring computational effort on Ethereum. Metaphor for fuel. NOT chemical/physical gas.

gas limit.definition: The maximum amount of gas a transaction or block may consume.

gas price.definition: Price in ether of one unit of gas specified in a transaction.

gwei.definition: Short for gigawei, a denomination of ether, commonly utilized to price gas. 1 gwei = 109 wei. 10

internal transaction.definition: A transaction sent from a contract account to another contract account or an EOA (see

max fee per gas.definition: The Max Fee is the absolute maximum amount a user is willing to pay per unit of gas (gwei) to get a transaction included in a block.

mempool.translation_note: Pool of pending transactions waiting to be included in a block.

nonce.definition: In cryptography, a value that can only be used once. An account nonce is a transaction counter in each account, which is used to prevent replay attacks.
nonce.translation_note: A counter ensuring each transaction is unique. Cryptographic term: 'number used once'.

receipt.definition: Data returned by an Ethereum client to represent the result of a particular transaction, including a hash of the transaction, its
receipt.translation_note: A record of a transaction's execution result. Technical blockchain term.

tip.translation_note: Priority fee paid to validators for faster inclusion. NOT a gratuity tip.

transaction.definition: Data committed to the Ethereum Blockchain signed by an originating account, targeting a specific address. The transaction contains met...
transaction.translation_note: A cryptographically signed data submission to the Ethereum blockchain. Not a financial transaction in the traditional sense.

transaction fee.definition: A fee you need to pay whenever you use the Ethereum network. Examples include sending funds from your wallet or a dapp interaction, like s...

wei.definition: The smallest denomination of ether. 10<sup>18</sup> wei = 1 ether.
wei.note: Denomination of ether (lowercase). Named after Wei Dai (proper noun when referring to the person).

stake.note: Arabic: verbal noun vs agent noun use distinct patterns. JP/KO: noun is loanword, verb needs native helper

sign.note: Romance languages require gender agreement for signed/signature

validate.note: CN/VI: technical 'validation' vs 'validator' role uses different semantic registers

bridge.note: Physical bridge noun vs functional/computing verb; HU/FI agglutinative suffixes change

mint.note: Lacks direct financial equivalent in non-Western languages

swap.note: DE/NL: noun (Tausch) and verb (tauschen) differ; DeFi context needs technical synonyms

fork.note: Often loanword as noun but translated as 'divergence' as verb in RU/PL

vote.note: Governance: 'ballot'(n) vs 'act of voting'(v) use different stems in Finno-Ugric

hash.note: Usually loanword noun; verb often 'calculate the checksum' in formal docs

sync.note: Long formal word for noun vs short English-derived slang for verb/action

attest.note: Ethereum 'attestation' is a specific object; general verb confuses with legal witnessing

propose.note: 'Block proposer' translates differently than general 'to propose an idea'

verify.note: ZK contexts distinguish heavily between prover and verifier; must be non-interchangeable

slash.note: Metaphorical penalty; action vs state require different idioms in TH/HI

build.note: 'Block builder' is specialized MEV role; 'constructing' vs 'software building' differ

withdraw.note: Action vs resulting transaction are grammatically distinct in Arabic

finalize.note: 'Finality' is chain property; 'finalized' is block state; must distinguish from 'finishing'

chain.note: Prefixes 'on-'/'off-' become complex prepositional phrases in TR/HU

claim.note: Action(v) to right(n) to status(adj); each needs specific phrasing in Asian languages
